caveats: a: Occupation evidence is broad production-work evidence rather than a direct task-exposure measure for 321991.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized by a cross-code median divisor.
rho: No representative five-year adoption study was found for manufactured-home plants.
rho: The estimate excludes standalone robotics unless AI directly enables the exposed task opportunity.
e: The 63-firm count is a margin-bridged estimate, not an observed list of qualifying manufacturers.
e: Repeat orders do not automatically constitute recurring outsourced service, making the eligibility boundary judgment-sensitive.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes.
s5: BizBuySell is a broker-marketplace sample and its manufacturing transactions are generally below the stated EBITDA band.
q: No public contract sample was found for qualifying private firms.
q: The lens covers only repeat relationships, which may have stronger buyer bargaining power than one-off retail sales.
d5: Shipment counts are not adjusted for quality or configuration mix.
d5: The current series is national and does not isolate repeat outsourced-production customers.
o: Operator requirement is inferred from current regulation over a five-year horizon.
o: The estimate allows limited self-supply or vertical integration but not elimination of physical production.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Homes are sold per unit or project, generally through dealer and developer channels; recurring revenue arises only where customers repeatedly outsource production under an ongoing supplier relationship.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is product manufacturing rather than a recurring service. For coherence, the lens retains only manufacturers with repeat outsourced-production relationships and excludes one-off unit sellers.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off direct-to-consumer product sales, retailers, installers, transporters, captive plants, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufactured-home producers only where repeat dealer, developer, community-owner, or institutional orders create an ongoing outsourced production relationship.

a.bridge: BLS describes production occupations as operating machinery and equipment to assemble goods; HUD describes plant construction, approved quality control, inspections, and certification. I translate that physical-and-regulated work mix into a small exposed share concentrated in estimating, drafting support, scheduling, procurement, documentation, and customer administration. This may understate computer-vision and robotics-adjacent AI gains but may overstate substitution where tasks remain embodied.
caveats: Occupation evidence is broad production-work evidence rather than a direct task-exposure measure for 321991.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized by a cross-code median divisor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted OEWS staffing table specific to NAICS 321991 combined with task-level time studies at several manufactured-home plants.
a.rationale: Most direct labor builds, moves, wires, plumbs, inspects, and finishes large physical units. Generative and predictive AI can reduce office and coordination work and assist design and quality records, but little of core assembly is substitutable by software alone.

d5.bridge: Census/FRED shipment data measure physical new HUD-code home shipments, not constant-quality demand from qualifying repeat customers. I use current shipment levels and the survey's complete HUD-code coverage as a volume anchor, then apply a broad five-year band for housing-cycle and financing sensitivity. Shipments omit modular homes and do not isolate LMM recurring-channel manufacturers, so the proxy can move differently from lens demand.
caveats: Shipment counts are not adjusted for quality or configuration mix.
The current series is national and does not isolate repeat outsourced-production customers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year unit forecast from Census/HUD shipment microdata linked to dealer inventories, placements, financing approvals, and customer type, expressed on a constant-specification basis.
d5.rationale: Manufactured homes address persistent affordable-housing needs, but demand is cyclical and exposed to rates, consumer financing, land availability, zoning, and dealer inventory. A near-flat real-volume base with a wide range is warranted.

caveats: The 63-firm count is a margin-bridged estimate, not an observed list of qualifying manufacturers.
Repeat orders do not automatically constitute recurring outsourced service, making the eligibility boundary judgment-sensitive.
e.change_evidence: Customer-level revenue histories and contract reviews for the 63 dataset-estimated LMM firms, classifying repeat outsourced programs versus transactional unit sales.
e.rationale: Most establishments sell a capital product rather than a recurring outsourced service, so they fail the frozen lens. A minority may have repeat dealer, developer, community, or institutional programs sufficiently relationship-based to qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321991 ($1,978,967,475) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321991 ($7,572,649,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $236,335 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321991); class <5: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,041 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,753 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,065,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $302,645 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,738,360 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $547,670 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,477,974 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,974,523 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,904,542 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,285,515 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $532,664,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $78,035,369 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 63

o.bridge: HUD directly requires manufacturer certification, approved quality control, factory inspection, and remediation for HUD-code homes. I map that regulatory accountability to the share of future quantity still needing an accountable manufacturer-operator. This could overstate the need for the current firm type if vertically integrated developers or highly automated plants internalize production.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from current regulation over a five-year horizon.
The estimate allows limited self-supply or vertical integration but not elimination of physical production.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on developer or dealer vertical integration, factory consolidation, lights-out production, and regulatory changes that shift certification responsibility away from manufacturers.
o.rationale: AI can streamline design, planning, and records, but physical homes still must be manufactured, certified, inspected, transported, and corrected when defective. Software alone cannot supply the current service basket.

caveats: No public contract sample was found for qualifying private firms.
The lens covers only repeat relationships, which may have stronger buyer bargaining power than one-off retail sales.
q.change_evidence: Five years of customer-contract, quote, gross-margin, and renewal data separating fixed-price programs from indexed or cost-plus arrangements.
q.rationale: Per-unit and project pricing lets a producer initially retain internal efficiency, but dealer/developer negotiation, model repricing, competitive bidding, and renewal cycles pass part of the gain to customers. Regulatory compliance and differentiated quality can slow complete pass-through.

caveats: No representative five-year adoption study was found for manufactured-home plants.
The estimate excludes standalone robotics unless AI directly enables the exposed task opportunity.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level software adoption surveys and before/after implementation data for ERP copilots, automated estimating, design-rule checking, and computer-vision quality systems.
rho.rationale: Within the limited exposed pool, scheduling, quoting, purchasing, knowledge retrieval, and document generation are implementable in five years. Adoption is constrained by legacy plant systems, model-specific engineering, error costs, workforce change, and HUD quality-control obligations.

s5.bridge: Gallup measured five-year ownership-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners, while BizBuySell reports realized transactions for small manufacturing businesses. I use these broader small-business populations as the prior for eligible LMM manufactured-home producers, widening upward for aging private ownership and downward for capital intensity and strategic-buyer concentration. Marketplace deals skew smaller than the target band, so they likely overstate liquidity for this lens.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes.
BizBuySell is a broker-marketplace sample and its manufacturing transactions are generally below the stated EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook or Capital IQ control-deal census for NAICS 321991 plus owner-age and succession interviews covering the identified LMM firm universe.
s5.rationale: Owner succession creates a real transfer pool, and manufacturing businesses do transact, but this niche has few firms, large facilities, working-capital needs, regulatory approvals, and possible strategic ownership that reduce qualifying control-transfer frequency.

principal_driver: Persistent need for lower-cost housing combined with improvable planning, quoting, and compliance workflows.
principal_weakness: Very few product manufacturers clearly satisfy the recurring outsourced-service lens, and core labor is physical rather than software-substitutable.
